vitals.location: Manali to Leh, Himachal Pradesh / Ladakh, India
vitals.date: 2026: unconfirmed — no verified edition since 2015, promoter status unclear
vitals.field_size: 63 riders (last independently verified edition, 2015); promoter claims 100+ for a disputed 2022 edition
vitals.cutoff_time: Multi-stage event (9 stages in the last verified 2015 edition, not a fixed 7-day cutoff)

terrain.surface: Predominantly paved high-altitude highway, interrupted by sand, gravel and rocky stretches

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 79. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 73.

course_description.character: Starts in Manali, crosses from Himachal Pradesh over the Great Himalaya into the Trans-Himalayan terrain of Ladakh, passes through Leh, and finishes atop Khardung La. It covers roughly 500-510 km of the Manali-Leh-Khardung La corridor over multiple stages (nine in the last independently verified 2015 edition — not a fixed seven-day cutoff, despite marketing copy suggesting one). The surface is predominantly paved high-altitude highway, interrupted by sand, gravel and rocky stretches. Repeated high passes, large daily altitude changes and a final ascent above 5,300 m define it — this is a supported high-altitude endurance/expedition race, not a conventional one-day mass-start road event, and the promoter runs a competitive timed format alongside a parallel untimed adventure-tour option for the same route.
course_description.signature_challenge: The Leh-Khardung La finale, finishing around 5,359-5,375 m, where hypoxia compounds the final climb after days of accumulated high-altitude fatigue.
suffering_zones: Rohtang Pass (~13,000 ft / ~3,960 m) — the initial crossing from green Manali into the harsher, drier Lahaul terrain
The central high-pass sequence — four passes reportedly exceeding 15,000 ft (~4,570 m), with relentless gradient and altitude swings
Mixed-surface highway sectors — paved road interrupted by sand, gravel and rocky sections at altitude

logistics.lodging_strategy: Mix of hotel stays in Manali and Leh, with camping/dome tents and Swiss tents at high-altitude base camps during the multi-stage event. Post-race accommodation in Leh hotels.

history.origin_story: Organized by Northern Escapes Outdoors Pvt. Ltd. (promoter Gaurav Schimar); Cycling 4 Change/Anil Uchil co-organized the last independently verified edition in 2015, which produced named men's and women's winners from a 63-rider field over nine stages.

biased_opinion.summary: This is a genuinely demanding high-altitude, multi-stage cycling event on the Manali-Leh-Khardung La corridor — but its current operating status cannot be confirmed. The last independently verifiable edition finished on 3 August 2015 with a real 63-rider field and named winners. The promoter (Northern Escapes Outdoors) has since advertised further editions, including a claimed 2022 running and a 2024 edition scheduled for 7-16 August, but no independent source confirms either actually took place — the official site itself has remained frozen on the 2024 dates with no post-event report. Promoter marketing also overstates the route: distance is claimed at 558.4 km against the promoter's own more conservative 500-510 km figure, and Khardung La is still billed as the 'world's highest motorable pass,' a claim Guinness World Records no longer supports (that title now belongs to Umling La at 5,798 m). Treat every current-year claim from this promoter with real skepticism until a 2026 edition is independently verified.
strengths: A real competitive edition existed: the verified 2015 running produced named winners, a 63-rider field and medical support with staged acclimatization
Genuinely exceptional route — sustained high-altitude endurance racing across the Great Himalaya into Trans-Himalayan Ladakh, finishing above 5,300 m
Progressive multi-day staging designed for acclimatization rather than a single reckless push to altitude
weaknesses: No independent confirmation of any edition since 2015 — the promoter's own site remains frozen on 2024 dates with no verified post-event report, and 2026 status is unconfirmed
Distance is inflated in promoter marketing (558.4 km claimed vs. the promoter's own 500-510 km figure elsewhere) and elevation gain is unverified

Combines timed racing and untimed touring under one banner, described by a past winner as a 'nine-day MTB marathon' — riders should not assume this is a straightforward one-day mass-start road championship
The 'world's highest motorable road/pass' claim attached to Khardung La is outdated; Guinness now recognizes Umling La (5,798 m) for that title
biased_opinion.bottom_line: If you can independently confirm a current edition is actually running — start with the promoter directly, not just their website — this is a genuinely extreme high-altitude test. Until then, treat 2026 registration claims with skepticism; the last verifiable finish line was in 2015.

final_verdict.one_liner: An extreme Himalayan high-altitude stage race on paper — but unverified since 2015, so confirm it's actually running before you book.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Only after independently confirming a current edition is scheduled and has run recently — the promoter's marketing has outpaced verifiable results for over a decade.
final_verdict.alternatives: For verified, currently-active extreme high-altitude cycling in the same region, look at organized Manali-Leh or Khardung La cycling expeditions run by established Himalayan adventure-tourism operators with recent, documented departures.

citations.label: Promoter Site — Frozen on 2024 Dates, No Verified Post-Event Report
Daily Excelsior — Last Independently Verified Winner Report (2015)
Guinness World Records — Highest Road Is Now Umling La, Not Khardung La

eligibility.notes: SUPERSEDES prior 'active' status. No 2025 or 2026 edition independently confirmed; official site remains frozen on 7-16 August 2024 dates with no independent report that edition occurred. Last independently verified finish: 3 August 2015 (63-rider field, named winners). Promoter alone claims a 2022 edition. Verified 2026-07-24 via codex gpt-5.6-sol foreground research as part of the Roadie Labs editorial wave 3.

catalog_flags.status_note: fondo_rating.discipline is 'gran_fondo' but this is a multi-stage event (9 stages in the last verified 2015 edition) combining a timed race with a parallel untimed adventure tour — doesn't cleanly fit the gran_fondo taxonomy or its single-day scoring dimensions. Recommend a human call on reclassifying discipline or adding scoring caveats, consistent with how flandrien-ride.json and birkebeinerrittet-road.json flag similar taxonomy mismatches rather than silently changing the discipline field.
